request.question: what year was the 2008 electee for a Cheshire constituency represented in the UK Parliament born?

title: Dartford (UK Parliament constituency)
North Wiltshire (UK Parliament constituency)
John Lloyd Wharton
Pendle (UK Parliament constituency)
Crewe and Nantwich (UK Parliament constituency)
Harrogate (UK Parliament constituency)
West Suffolk (UK Parliament constituency)
Stalybridge (UK Parliament constituency)
Edward Timpson
Mansfield (UK Parliament constituency)
sentences: Dartford is a constituency represented in the House of Commons of the UK Parliament since 2010 by Gareth Johnson of the Conservative Party.
The constituency is currently the longest-valid 'bellwether' constituency in the country as the party of the winning candidate has gone on to form the government in every General Election since 1964.
Candidates for the largest two parties nationally have polled first and second since 1923 in Dartford.
North Wiltshire is a constituency represented in the House of Commons of the UK Parliament since 1997 by James Gray, a Conservative.

In the period 1832–1983, this was an alternative name for Chippenham or the Northern Division of Wiltshire and as Chippenham dates to the original countrywide Parliament, the Model Parliament, this period is covered in more detail in that article.
In 2016 it was announced that the North Wiltshire constituency would be scrapped as part of the planned 2018 Constituency Reforms.
The Right Honourable John Lloyd Wharton PC (18 April 1837 – 11 July 1912) was a Barrister and a Conservative Party politician.
He was the Member of Parliament (MP) for Member of Parliament (MP) for City of Durham (UK Parliament constituency) then Member of Parliament (MP) for Ripon (UK Parliament constituency).
Pendle is a constituency represented in the House of Commons of the UK Parliament since 2010 by Andrew Stephenson, a Conservative.
The constituency was newly created for the 1983 General Election, being largely formed from the former Nelson and Colne Constituency.
Crewe and Nantwich is a constituency in Cheshire represented in the House of Commons of the UK Parliament since 2017, by Laura Smith of the Labour Party.
Harrogate ( ) was a constituency represented in the House of Commons of the UK Parliament.
As with all constituencies, the constituency elected one Member of Parliament (MP) by the first past the post system of election.
The constituency was renamed Harrogate and Knaresborough in 1997.
West Suffolk is a constituency represented in the House of Commons of the UK Parliament since 2010 by Matthew Hancock, a Conservative.
Between 1832 and 1885 there had also been a constituency, the Western Division of Suffolk that had also been called West Suffolk.
Stalybridge officially sometimes written in early years as Staleybridge was a constituency represented in the House of Commons of the UK Parliament from 1868 until 1918 by one MP.
It comprised the borough of Stalybridge which lay in Lancashire and Cheshire and which is in the east of today's Greater Manchester.
On abolition for the 1918 general election under the Representation of the People Act 1918 the seat's main replacement became Stalybridge and Hyde.
Anthony Edward Timpson (born (1973--)26 1973 ) is a British Conservative Party politician.
He was first elected as the Member of Parliament (MP) for Crewe and Nantwich at the 2008 by-election but lost his seat in the 2017 general election to the Labour candidate, Laura Smith.
Mansfield is a constituency represented in the House of Commons of the UK Parliament since 2017 by Ben Bradley of the Conservative Party.
This is the first time a Conservative MP has represented the constituency since its creation in 1885.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

hop1_docs: Eddisbury (UK Parliament constituency) | Eddisbury is a constituency in Cheshire represented in the House of Commons of the UK Parliament since 2015 by Antoinette Sandbach, a Conservative.
Congleton (UK Parliament constituency) | Congleton is a constituency in Cheshire, represented in the House of Commons of the UK Parliament since 2010 by Fiona Bruce of the Conservative Party.
Halton (UK Parliament constituency) | Halton is a constituency in Cheshire, represented in the House of Commons of the UK Parliament since 1997 by Derek Twigg of the Labour Party.

Crewe and Nantwich (UK Parliament constituency) | Crewe and Nantwich is a constituency in Cheshire represented in the House of Commons of the UK Parliament since 2017, by Laura Smith of the Labour Party.

Cheshire (UK Parliament constituency) | Cheshire is a former United Kingdom Parliamentiary constituency for the county of Cheshire.  It was a constituency of the House of Commons of the Parliament of England then of the Parliament of Great Britain from 1707 to 1800 and of the Parliament of the United Kingdom from 1801 to 1832.

Stalybridge (UK Parliament constituency) | Stalybridge officially sometimes written in early years as Staleybridge was a constituency represented in the House of Commons of the UK Parliament from 1868 until 1918 by one MP.  It comprised the borough of Stalybridge which lay in Lancashire and Cheshire and which is in the east of today's Greater Manchester.  On abolition for the 1918 general election under the Representation of the People Act 1918 the seat's main replacement became Stalybridge and Hyde.

Hyde (UK Parliament constituency) | Hyde was a county constituency represented in the House of Commons of the Parliament of the United Kingdom from 1885 until 1918.  It was seated in the town of Hyde, Cheshire.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - "Eddisbury (UK Parliament constituency)" — passage [1]: "represented ... since 2015 by Antoinette Sandbach" (quote from passage [1]).
  - "Congleton (UK Parliament constituency)" — passage [2]: "since 2010 by Fiona Bruce" (quote from passage [2]).
  - "Halton (UK Parliament constituency)" — passage [3]: "since 1997 by Derek Twigg" (quote from passage [3]).
  - "Crewe and Nantwich (UK Parliament constituency)" — passage [4]: "since 2017, by Laura Smith" (quote from passage [4]).
- Ambiguities:
  - The question does not name which Cheshire constituency had a 2008 electee. Passages list multiple candidate constituency pages: "Eddisbury", "Congleton", "Halton", "Crewe and Nantwich". None of these passages mention "2008" or a 2008 electee; the expected 2008 electee page/variant is missing from the provided passages.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entity candidates (as they appear in the passages): "Eddisbury (UK Parliament constituency)"; "Congleton (UK Parliament constituency)"; "Halton (UK Parliament constituency)"; "Crewe and Nantwich (UK Parliament constituency)".
  - Precise missing fact needed: the name of the MP elected in 2008 for the relevant Cheshire constituency and that MP's year of birth.
- Retrieval direction (prioritized):
  1) Open "Crewe and Nantwich (UK Parliament constituency)" page — check the lede/infobox and the "Elections" / "By-elections" / "History" sections for any 2008 election and the MP elected then.
     - Example search keywords: "Crewe and Nantwich 2008 by-election elected 2008".
  2) If not found, open "Congleton (UK Parliament constituency)" and "Eddisbury (UK Parliament constituency)" pages — check lede/infobox and "Elections"/"History" sections for 2008 election entries.
     - Example keywords: "Congleton 2008 elected", "Eddisbury 2008".
  3) Open "Halton (UK Parliament constituency)" page similarly if needed.
  4) If constituency pages do not show a 2008 electee, fetch a central list page such as "List of United Kingdom by-elections" or "2008 United Kingdom by-elections" and then open the MP's individual page to read the lede/infobox for the birth year.
Final answer deferred until those pages and the MP's page(s) are retrieved.
hop2_docs: Crewe and Nantwich by-election, 2008 | The Crewe and Nantwich by-election, 2008 was a parliamentary by-election held on 22 May 2008, for the British House of Commons constituency of Crewe and Nantwich, in Cheshire, England.  The election was won by the Conservative party candidate Edward Timpson, who defeated the Labour party candidate Tamsin Dunwoody, on a swing from Labour to Conservative of 17.6%, a swing that in a general election would see nine Labour cabinet ministers lose their seats.  At the time of the by-election, a swing of 7% in a general election would have seen the Conservatives gain an overall majority over Labour.  This was the first seat gained by the Conservatives in a by-election since the Mitcham and Morden by-election, 1982.
Crewe and Nantwich Borough Council elections | Crewe and Nantwich was a non-metropolitan district in Cheshire, England.  It was abolished on 1 April 2009 and replaced by Cheshire East.

Edward Timpson | Anthony Edward Timpson (born (1973--)26 1973 ) is a British Conservative Party politician.  He was first elected as the Member of Parliament (MP) for Crewe and Nantwich at the 2008 by-election but lost his seat in the 2017 general election to the Labour candidate, Laura Smith.
Nantwich Rural District | Nantwich Rural District was a division of Cheshire until 1974, when it merged with the Nantwich and Crewe Urban districts to create the borough of Crewe and Nantwich.

Crewe and Nantwich Circular Walk | The Crewe and Nantwich Circular Walk is a 29 mi long-distance walkers' path in the Cheshire East area of Cheshire, England.  As the name suggests, the walk forms a circuit around the towns of Crewe and Nantwich.  It is one of two circular walks in the county of Cheshire, the other being the Vale Royal Round.

Crewe & Nantwich Steamers | Crewe & Nantwich Steamers is a rugby league club in Nantwich, Cheshire.  They play in the North West Division of the Rugby League Conference and run a second team in the North West Merit League.

Nantwich railway station | Nantwich railway station serves the town of Nantwich, Cheshire, England.  It is on the Crewe to Shrewsbury line 4+1/2 mi south west of Crewe.  Opened in 1858, it was the junction for the Great Western Railway route to Wellington via Market Drayton until 1963.
hop2_titles: Crewe and Nantwich Borough Council elections
response.summary_2: 1973. Anthony Edward Timpson is a British Conservative Party politician who was first elected as MP for Crewe and Nantwich in the 2008 by-election.
